([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): [吴](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%90%B4 "wiktionary:吴")[蛮](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%9B%AE "wiktionary:蛮"); [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Wú Mán*; born January 2, 1963)[[1]](#cite_note-1) is a Chinese [pipa](/wiki/Pipa "Pipa") player and composer. Trained in [Pudong-style](/wiki/Pipa#Schools "Pipa") pipa performance at the [Central Conservatory of Music](/wiki/Central_Conservatory_of_Music "Central Conservatory of Music") in Beijing, she is known for playing in a broad range of musical styles and introducing the pipa and its Chinese heritage into Western genres. She has performed and recorded extensively with the [Kronos Quartet](/wiki/Kronos_Quartet "Kronos Quartet") and [Silk Road Ensemble](/wiki/Silk_Road_Ensemble "Silk Road Ensemble") and has premiered works by [Philip Glass](/wiki/Philip_Glass "Philip Glass"), [Lou Harrison](/wiki/Lou_Harrison "Lou Harrison"), [Terry Riley](/wiki/Terry_Riley "Terry Riley"), [Bright Sheng](/wiki/Bright_Sheng "Bright Sheng"), [Tan Dun](/wiki/Tan_Dun "Tan Dun"), [Zhao Jiping](/wiki/Zhao_Jiping "Zhao Jiping"), and [Zhou Long](/wiki/Zhou_Long "Zhou Long"), among many others. She has recorded and appeared on over 40 albums, five of which have been nominated for [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards"). In 2013, she was named Instrumentalist of the Year by *[Musical America](/wiki/Musical_America "Musical America")*, becoming the first performer of a non-Western instrument to receive this award.[[2]](#cite_note-Musical_America-2) She additionally received The [United States Artists](/wiki/United_States_Artists "United States Artists") Award in 2008.

Musical artist

**Wu Man** ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): [吴](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%90%B4 "wiktionary:吴")[蛮](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%9B%AE "wiktionary:蛮"); [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Wú Mán*; born January 2, 1963)[[1]](#cite_note-1) is a Chinese [pipa](/wiki/Pipa "Pipa") player and composer. Trained in [Pudong-style](/wiki/Pipa#Schools "Pipa") pipa performance at the [Central Conservatory of Music](/wiki/Central_Conservatory_of_Music "Central Conservatory of Music") in Beijing, she is known for playing in a broad range of musical styles and introducing the pipa and its Chinese heritage into Western genres. She has performed and recorded extensively with the [Kronos Quartet](/wiki/Kronos_Quartet "Kronos Quartet") and [Silk Road Ensemble](/wiki/Silk_Road_Ensemble "Silk Road Ensemble") and has premiered works by [Philip Glass](/wiki/Philip_Glass "Philip Glass"), [Lou Harrison](/wiki/Lou_Harrison "Lou Harrison"), [Terry Riley](/wiki/Terry_Riley "Terry Riley"), [Bright Sheng](/wiki/Bright_Sheng "Bright Sheng"), [Tan Dun](/wiki/Tan_Dun "Tan Dun"), [Zhao Jiping](/wiki/Zhao_Jiping "Zhao Jiping"), and [Zhou Long](/wiki/Zhou_Long "Zhou Long"), among many others. She has recorded and appeared on over 40 albums, five of which have been nominated for [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards"). In 2013, she was named Instrumentalist of the Year by *[Musical America](/wiki/Musical_America "Musical America")*, becoming the first performer of a non-Western instrument to receive this award.[[2]](#cite_note-Musical_America-2) She additionally received The [United States Artists](/wiki/United_States_Artists "United States Artists") Award in 2008.

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wu_Man&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

Born in [Hangzhou](/wiki/Hangzhou "Hangzhou"), [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang"), Wu Man began taking pipa lessons at age 9. When universities opened their doors to new students in 1977 after the [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution") had ended, Wu Man traveled to Beijing to audition for the [Central Conservatory of Music](/wiki/Central_Conservatory_of_Music "Central Conservatory of Music"). At 13, she became the youngest student at the school, and her final audition was covered in national newspapers.[[3]](#cite_note-Chinese_Idol-3) While at the Conservatory, she studied [Pudong-style](/wiki/Pipa#Schools "Pipa") pipa performance under [Lin Shicheng](/wiki/Pipa#Modern_era "Pipa"), Kuang Yuzhong, Chen Zemin, and [Liu Dehai](/wiki/Liu_Dehai "Liu Dehai").[[4]](#cite_note-4) She was first exposed to non-Chinese music in 1979 when [Seiji Ozawa](/wiki/Seiji_Ozawa "Seiji Ozawa") and the [Boston Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Boston_Symphony_Orchestra "Boston Symphony Orchestra") performed in Beijing, and again in 1980 when she attended [Isaac Stern](/wiki/Isaac_Stern "Isaac Stern")’s master classes at the Conservatory.[[3]](#cite_note-Chinese_Idol-3) She received her master's degree (the first such degree conferred on a pipa player) in 1987.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Wu Man first performed in the United States as a member of the China Youth Arts Troupe in 1985. She then moved to the U.S. five years later to pursue a career in pipa performance that would allow her repertoire to extend beyond traditional Chinese music.[[3]](#cite_note-Chinese_Idol-3) Upon arriving in [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut") with her husband (who had begun working on his Ph.D. in chemistry at [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University")), she began working with ensembles such as the [Kronos Quartet](/wiki/Kronos_Quartet "Kronos Quartet") ([see below](#Kronos_Quartet)), and premiered works by Chinese and Chinese-American composers. In 1997, she gave the premiere of [Lou Harrison](/wiki/Lou_Harrison "Lou Harrison")'s Concerto for Pipa and String Orchestra with [Dennis Russell Davies](/wiki/Dennis_Russell_Davies "Dennis Russell Davies") conducting the [Stuttgart Chamber Orchestra](/wiki/Stuttgart_Chamber_Orchestra "Stuttgart Chamber Orchestra") at [Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts](/wiki/Lincoln_Center_for_the_Performing_Arts "Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts"). Written specifically for Wu Man, the work has been recorded twice, the latter of which (with the [Chicago Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Chicago_Symphony_Orchestra "Chicago Symphony Orchestra") and [Miguel Harth-Bedoya](/wiki/Miguel_Harth-Bedoya "Miguel Harth-Bedoya")) was nominated for a Grammy for Best Instrumental Soloist Performance with Orchestra.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-2009_Grammy-7) In 1998, Wu Man was awarded a fellowship to study at the [Bunting Institute at Radcliffe College](/wiki/Radcliffe_Institute_for_Advanced_Study "Radcliffe Institute for Advanced Study").[[8]](#cite_note-Bunting-8) That same year, she became a founding member of [Yo-Yo Ma](/wiki/Yo-Yo_Ma "Yo-Yo Ma")'s [Silk Road Project](/wiki/Silk_Road_Project "Silk Road Project") ([see below](#Yo-Yo_Ma_and_the_Silk_Road_Project)).[[9]](#cite_note-SFCV-9)

In 2003, Wu Man began working with [Philip Glass](/wiki/Philip_Glass "Philip Glass"), performing in the premiere of his opera *[The Sound of a Voice](/wiki/The_Sound_of_a_Voice "The Sound of a Voice")* at the [American Repertory Theater](/wiki/American_Repertory_Theater "American Repertory Theater").[[10]](#cite_note-10) She is featured in the recording of a suite from this work, which was released in 2007 on Glass's private label, Orange Mountain Music.[[11]](#cite_note-Theater_Music-11) In 2004, Wu Man was approached by Glass once again to collaborate on a commission from the [Cultural Olympiad](/wiki/Cultural_Olympiad "Cultural Olympiad") on the occasion of the [2004 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2004_Summer_Olympics "2004 Summer Olympics") in Athens. Taking [Orion](/wiki/Orion_(constellation) "Orion (constellation)") as its title and inspiration (the constellation can be seen from both hemispheres throughout the year), the piece contains movements representing various countries around the world. The third movement, "China", was co-written by Glass and Wu Man, and features Wu Man on pipa. Additional collaborators include [Ravi Shankar](/wiki/Ravi_Shankar "Ravi Shankar"), [Mark Atkins](/wiki/Mark_Atkins_(musician) "Mark Atkins (musician)"), [Foday Musa Suso](/wiki/Foday_Musa_Suso "Foday Musa Suso"), [Ashley MacIsaac](/wiki/Ashley_MacIsaac "Ashley MacIsaac"), and [Uakti](/wiki/Uakti_(band) "Uakti (band)"). The work was premiered prior to the Games, and recorded and released on Orange Mountain Music in 2005.[[12]](#cite_note-:0-12)

In 2009, Wu Man curated a pair of concerts at [Carnegie Hall](/wiki/Carnegie_Hall "Carnegie Hall") as part of the *Ancient Paths, Modern Voices* Festival. The concerts focused on music from remote areas of China, including traditions of the [Dong](/wiki/Dong_people "Dong people") and [Tujia](/wiki/Tujia_people "Tujia people") peoples, as well as [Taoist](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism") ritual.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The process of finding these musicians was documented in the 2012 film *Discovering a Musical Heartland: Wu Man's Return to China*.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Wu Man's achievements in bringing the pipa to new audiences were recognized at the end of 2012 when *[Musical America](/wiki/Musical_America "Musical America")* selected her as the 2013 Instrumentalist of the Year. She is the first performer of a non-Western instrument to receive this award.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Later that season, Wu Man premiered her composition *Blue and Green* with New York–based chamber orchestra The Knights. Arranged for pipa and string orchestra by [Lev Zhurbin](/wiki/Lev_Zhurbin "Lev Zhurbin") and The Knights' Colin Jacobsen, the work is influenced by folk melodies that Wu Man heard travelling through China.[[16]](#cite_note-16) That same season, Wu Man premiered [Zhao Jiping](/wiki/Zhao_Jiping "Zhao Jiping")’s Pipa Concerto No. 2 at the [Sydney Opera House](/wiki/Sydney_Opera_House "Sydney Opera House") with the [Sydney Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Sydney_Symphony_Orchestra "Sydney Symphony Orchestra"), who commissioned the work along with a consortium of North American orchestras.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In 2014, Wu Man was named the first Musician-in-Residence at the [Huntington Library](/wiki/Huntington_Library "Huntington Library"). The residency included educational outreach programs, as well as a commission for a new composition. The piece, *Three Sharing*, was written by Wu Man for pipa, [janggo](/wiki/Janggo "Janggo"), and [shakuhachi](/wiki/Shakuhachi "Shakuhachi"), and was premiered on June 17, 2014 at the Huntington's [Chinese Garden](/wiki/Huntington_Library#Chinese_Garden_.28Liu_Fang_Yuan_.E6.B5.81.E8.8A.B3.E5.9C.92.29 "Huntington Library").[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Collaborations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wu_Man&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Collaborations")]

### Kronos Quartet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wu_Man&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Kronos Quartet")]

Wu Man first performed with the [Kronos Quartet](/wiki/Kronos_Quartet "Kronos Quartet") in the premiere of [Zhou Long](/wiki/Zhou_Long "Zhou Long")'s *Soul* for pipa and string quartet at the [Pittsburgh New Music Festival](/wiki/Pittsburgh_New_Music_Ensemble "Pittsburgh New Music Ensemble") in 1992.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The Quartet's founding violinist, David Harrington, says he "heard all sorts of possibilities in Wu Man's vivid pipa sound,"[[21]](#cite_note-Chinese_Home-21) and the Quartet subsequently commissioned [Tan Dun](/wiki/Tan_Dun "Tan Dun") to write a piece for the same instrumentation. The resulting *Ghost Opera*, a semi-staged work with minimal sets and lighting, received its premiere at the [Brooklyn Academy of Music](/wiki/Brooklyn_Academy_of_Music "Brooklyn Academy of Music") in 1995.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The work was later [recorded](/wiki/Tan_Dun:_Ghost_Opera "Tan Dun: Ghost Opera") and released on [Nonesuch](/wiki/Nonesuch_Records "Nonesuch Records") in 1997.[[23]](#cite_note-Ghost_Opera_CD-23) On the occasion of [Terry Riley](/wiki/Terry_Riley "Terry Riley")'s 70th birthday in 2005, the Quartet commissioned a new work from him that would include pipa. After working with Wu Man for over a year to learn the intricacies of the instrument, Riley composed *The Cusp of Magic*, a work in six movements that included Wu Man on pipa and vocals. All five musicians also play a variety of percussion instruments and toys. The premiere was presented by Cal Performances at Hertz Hall on the [University of California, Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley") campus, and was recorded and released on Nonesuch in 2008.[[24]](#cite_note-Riley-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) Wu Man's most recent project with the Quartet is a multimedia work entitled *A Chinese Home*, co-conceived by Wu Man, Harrington, and Chinese theater director [Chen Shi-Zheng](/wiki/Chen_Shi-Zheng "Chen Shi-Zheng"). The work, which received its premiere at Carnegie's [Zankel Hall](/wiki/Carnegie_Hall#Zankel_Hall "Carnegie Hall") in 2009, depicts a broad stretch of Chinese history, from the [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") through the [Chinese Communist Revolution](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Revolution "Chinese Communist Revolution") to present day.[[21]](#cite_note-Chinese_Home-21)

Wu Man continues to perform regularly with Kronos and was a featured guest artist in the Quartet's 40th Anniversary concerts in the 2013-14 season at the International Festival of Arts & Ideas, Cal Performances, Center for the Art of Performance at [UCLA](/wiki/UCLA "UCLA"), and Carnegie Hall.[[26]](#cite_note-26) She appears on [five albums](#Kronos) with the Quartet, including *[Early Music](/wiki/Early_Music_(Lachrym%C3%A6_Antiqu%C3%A6) "Early Music (Lachrymæ Antiquæ)")*, in which she plays [John Dowland](/wiki/John_Dowland "John Dowland")'s *Lachrymæ Antiquæ* on the [ruan](/wiki/Ruan_(instrument) "Ruan (instrument)"); *[You've Stolen My Heart](/wiki/You%27ve_Stolen_My_Heart_(album) "You've Stolen My Heart (album)")*, a collection of [Bollywood songs](/wiki/Music_of_Bollywood "Music of Bollywood") by [Rahul Dev Burman](/wiki/Rahul_Dev_Burman "Rahul Dev Burman"); and *A Thousand Thoughts*, the Quartet's 40th anniversary compilation album.[[27]](#cite_note-Early_Music-27)[[28]](#cite_note-RD_Burman-28)[[29]](#cite_note-Kronos_1000-29)

### Yo-Yo Ma and the Silk Road Project

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wu_Man&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Yo-Yo Ma and the Silk Road Project")]

Wu Man is a founding member of [Yo-Yo Ma](/wiki/Yo-Yo_Ma "Yo-Yo Ma")'s [Silk Road Project](/wiki/Silk_Road_Project "Silk Road Project"), a non-profit organization established in 1998 to foster cross-cultural communication through musical performance and education.[[9]](#cite_note-SFCV-9)[[30]](#cite_note-30) Two years after its inception, the Project hosted a workshop at the [Tanglewood Music Center](/wiki/Tanglewood_Music_Center "Tanglewood Music Center") that evolved into the Silk Road Ensemble, an eclectic group of musicians representing countries along the [Silk Road](/wiki/Silk_Road "Silk Road").[[31]](#cite_note-31) Wu Man has performed regularly with the Ensemble since its inception in 2000, recording [five albums](#SRE) and touring internationally.

In the 2006-07 season, the Silk Road Project partnered with the [Chicago Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Chicago_Symphony_Orchestra "Chicago Symphony Orchestra") for a yearlong celebration called [Silk Road Chicago](/wiki/Silk_Road_Chicago "Silk Road Chicago"), culminating in the 2008 album release *Traditions and Transformations: Sounds of Silk Road Chicago*. Wu Man appears on this album as a soloist, performing [Lou Harrison](/wiki/Lou_Harrison "Lou Harrison")'s Pipa Concerto with the CSO and conductor [Miguel Harth-Bedoya](/wiki/Miguel_Harth-Bedoya "Miguel Harth-Bedoya").[[32]](#cite_note-SRE_discog-32)

Wu Man has also performed multiple times with Yo-Yo Ma outside of the Silk Road Ensemble. In 1999, Wu Man and Ma performed at the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House"), premiering [Bright Sheng](/wiki/Bright_Sheng "Bright Sheng")'s Three Songs for Pipa and Violoncello.[[33]](#cite_note-33) That same year, Ma was awarded the [Glenn Gould Prize](/wiki/Glenn_Gould_Prize "Glenn Gould Prize") and selected Wu Man as the Glenn Gould Protégé.[[34]](#cite_note-Glenn_Gould_Prize-34) They performed together again in 2003, premiering Sheng’s *The Song and Dance of Tears* alongside pianist [Emanuel Ax](/wiki/Emanuel_Ax "Emanuel Ax"), with [David Zinman](/wiki/David_Zinman "David Zinman") conducting the [New York Philharmonic](/wiki/New_York_Philharmonic "New York Philharmonic").[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### Miscellaneous cross-cultural collaborations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wu_Man&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Miscellaneous cross-cultural collaborations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Wu_Man_%28China%29_-_WOMEX_15%2C_2015.10.24_%281%29.JPG/250px-Wu_Man_%28China%29_-_WOMEX_15%2C_2015.10.24_%281%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Wu_Man_(China)_-_WOMEX_15,_2015.10.24_(1).JPG)

Wu Man performing at WOMEX 15, Budapest

In 2005, Wu Man and composer [Chen Yi](/wiki/Chen_Yi_(composer) "Chen Yi (composer)") co-wrote a multimedia work titled *Ancient Dances*, commissioned by the [Walton Arts Center](/wiki/Walton_Arts_Center "Walton Arts Center") in [Fayetteville, Arkansas](/wiki/Fayetteville,_Arkansas "Fayetteville, Arkansas"). Taking its inspiration from the poetry of [Li Bai](/wiki/Li_Bai "Li Bai"), the multimedia work features video art by Kathleen Owen that incorporates [Chinese calligraphy](/wiki/Chinese_calligraphy "Chinese calligraphy") and [paintings](/wiki/Chinese_painting "Chinese painting") by Wu Man's father. In performances of the work, Wu Man and percussionist Robert Schulz played in front of two vertical screens that displayed Owen's art. The project forms the first installment in what Wu Man refers to as her larger "Return to the East" project, which includes many of the projects discussed below, as well as her documentary *Discovering a Musical Heartland*.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) Later in 2005, she recorded the album *Wu Man and Friends* with Lee Knight ([banjo](/wiki/Banjo "Banjo"), [Appalachian dulcimer](/wiki/Appalachian_dulcimer "Appalachian dulcimer"), [mouth bow](/wiki/Mouth_bow "Mouth bow"), vocals), [Julian Kytasty](/wiki/Julian_Kytasty "Julian Kytasty") ([bandura](/wiki/Bandura "Bandura"), [sopilka](/wiki/Sopilka "Sopilka"), vocals), and [James Makubuya](/wiki/James_Makubuya "James Makubuya") ([endongo](/wiki/Endongo "Endongo"), [adungu](/wiki/Adungu "Adungu"), vocals), which was released on the Traditional Crossroads label in 2006. The album contains the music of Chinese, [Appalachian](/wiki/Appalachian_music "Appalachian music"), [Ukrainian](/wiki/Music_of_Ukraine "Music of Ukraine"), and [Ugandan](/wiki/Music_of_Uganda "Music of Uganda") folk traditions, arranged to combine these plucked instruments from around the world.[[38]](#cite_note-Wu_Man_and_Friends-38) Since the album release, Wu Man, Knight, Kytasty, and Makubuya have performed their arrangements in concerts around the U.S., including Carnegie's [Zankel Hall](/wiki/Zankel_Hall "Zankel Hall") in 2006 and the [Santa Fe Chamber Music Festival](/wiki/Santa_Fe_Chamber_Music_Festival "Santa Fe Chamber Music Festival") in 2010.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)

In 2010, Wu Man traveled to Taiwan to study the music of [Taiwanese aborigines](/wiki/Taiwanese_aborigines "Taiwanese aborigines"), leading to a series of concerts in 2012 titled "Wu Man and Aboriginal Friends from Taiwan (吳蠻與原住民朋友)". In these concerts, Wu Man was joined onstage by musicians of the [Paiwan](/wiki/Paiwan_people "Paiwan people"), [Atayal](/wiki/Atayal_people "Atayal people"), and [Bunun](/wiki/Bunun_people "Bunun people") tribes, performing arrangements of traditional and ritual songs with pipa accompaniment. Performances took place at the Taiwan International Festival of Arts at the [National Theater and Concert Hall, Taipei](/wiki/National_Theater_and_Concert_Hall,_Taipei "National Theater and Concert Hall, Taipei"), and at the New Vision Arts Festival in Hong Kong.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)

With funds from the [Aga Khan Music Initiative in Central Asia](/wiki/Aga_Khan_Music_Initiative_in_Central_Asia "Aga Khan Music Initiative in Central Asia"), Wu Man collaborated with [Central Asian musicians](/wiki/Music_of_Central_Asia "Music of Central Asia") to produce the CD/DVD *Borderlands: Wu Man and Master Musicians from the Silk Route*, released on [Smithsonian Folkways](/wiki/Smithsonian_Folkways "Smithsonian Folkways") in 2012. The album/documentary forms the tenth and final installment in the record label's Music of Central Asia series. The music focuses heavily on the [Uyghur](/wiki/Uyghur_people "Uyghur people") [muqam](/wiki/Muqam "Muqam") tradition, a system of 12 modes that draws loosely from [Arabic maqam](/wiki/Arabic_maqam "Arabic maqam"). She is joined on the album by Abduvali Abdurashidov on [sato](/wiki/Sato_(instrument) "Sato (instrument)"); Sirojiddin Juraev on [dutar](/wiki/Dutar "Dutar"); [Hua'er](/wiki/Hua%27er "Hua'er") vocalist Ma Ersa; Abdulla Majnun on diltar (his own invention, combining the plucked [tanbur](/wiki/Tanbur "Tanbur") and bowed [satar](/wiki/Satar "Satar") into a single double-necked instrument), dutar, tanbur, and vocals; Hesenjan Tursun on [satar](/wiki/Setar#Uyghur_variant "Setar") and vocals; Sanubar Tursun on dutar and vocals; and Yasin Yaqup on [dap](/wiki/Daf "Daf").[[43]](#cite_note-43)

In 2013, Wu Man received an Individual Artist Fellowship from San Diego Foundation's Creative Catalyst Fund to pursue a project titled "When China Meets Latin America", collaborating with [son jarocho](/wiki/Son_jarocho "Son jarocho") quartet Son de San Diego. In their initial rehearsals, Wu Man and Son de San Diego explored the commonalities in Chinese and Latin American folk traditions, both of which, Son de San Diego's Eduardo Garcia Acosta notes, have "love songs, silvery dawns, birdsongs, broken hearts, tales of sailing, and the sheer joy of dancing."[[44]](#cite_note-44) Following these rehearsals, the *ad hoc* quintet has performed arrangements of Chinese and Latin American folk music in workshops and concerts presented by the Carlsbad Music Festival.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Wu Man performed in several concerts at the 2023 [Ojai Music Festival](/wiki/Ojai_Music_Festival "Ojai Music Festival"), including two performances of "Ghost Opera".

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wu_Man&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Wu Man lives with her husband, Peng Wang, in [Carlsbad](/wiki/Carlsbad,_California "Carlsbad, California"), [California](/wiki/California "California"), where they moved in 2004 after her husband accepted a job there.[[46]](#cite_note-sandiegouniontribune.com-46) She and her husband became naturalized U.S. citizens in 2000, two years after the birth of their son.[[46]](#cite_note-sandiegouniontribune.com-46)
